name: CGRP Therapies
description: Calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) monoclonal antibodies and gepants. Galcanezumab is the only approved anti-CGRP for episodic CH.

all_trials.title: A Study of LY2951742 (Galcanezumab) in Participants With Cluster Headache
all_trials.what_tested: Long-term safety of galcanezumab (Emgality) 300mg monthly injection in people who completed the earlier episodic or chronic CH trials.
all_trials.key_result: 119 of 165 participants experienced treatment-related adverse events, and 17 had serious adverse events. 2 participants reported suicidal ideation. No new safety signals beyond what was already known.
all_trials.patient_relevance: Galcanezumab appears reasonably safe for long-term use in CH patients, though side effects are common -- this was a safety study, not designed to prove it works.

all_trials.title: Role of Specific microRNAs in Cluster Headache
all_trials.sponsor: IRCCS National Neurological Institute "C. Mondino" Foundation
all_trials.what_tested: Investigating whether specific microRNAs (small genetic molecules) in blood cells differ between CH patients in active phase, remission, and healthy controls, along with measuring CGRP levels.
all_trials.key_result: Still recruiting 100 participants, expected completion December 2024.
all_trials.patient_relevance: This is basic research that could lead to a blood test for CH or help identify new drug targets -- no direct treatment implications yet.

all_trials.title: A Study Of Galcanezumab In Participants With Episodic Cluster Headache
all_trials.what_tested: Galcanezumab (Emgality) 300mg monthly injection versus placebo for preventing episodic cluster headache attacks.
all_trials.key_result: Galcanezumab reduced weekly attacks by 8.69 compared to 5.22 for placebo (difference of ~3.5 fewer attacks per week). This was a statistically significant result that led to some clinical use.
all_trials.patient_relevance: This is the study that made galcanezumab the first CGRP antibody to show benefit in episodic CH -- about 3-4 fewer attacks per week than placebo.

all_trials.title: A Study of Galcanezumab in Participants With Chronic Cluster Headache
all_trials.what_tested: Galcanezumab (Emgality) 300mg monthly injection versus placebo for preventing chronic cluster headache attacks.
all_trials.key_result: Galcanezumab reduced weekly attacks by 5.38 versus 4.59 for placebo -- a difference of less than 1 attack per week, which was not statistically significant.
all_trials.patient_relevance: Galcanezumab did NOT work for chronic CH -- the small improvement over placebo was not meaningful, which is disappointing since chronic patients need it most.

all_trials.title: A 1-year Trial to Inform About Long-term Exposure to Eptinezumab in Participants With Chronic Cluster Headache (cCH)
all_trials.what_tested: Long-term (1 year) safety of eptinezumab IV infusion in chronic cluster headache patients.
all_trials.key_result: 106 of 131 participants experienced adverse events, and 12 had serious adverse events. This was a safety extension study showing the drug was tolerable long-term.
all_trials.patient_relevance: Eptinezumab appears safe enough for long-term use in chronic CH, but this study was not designed to prove effectiveness.
all_trials.dose_tested: Eptinezumab IV infusion (dose not specified in summary)

all_trials.title: Eptinezumab in Participants With Episodic Cluster Headache
all_trials.what_tested: Eptinezumab (Vyepti) IV infusion versus placebo for preventing episodic cluster headache attacks.
all_trials.key_result: Eptinezumab reduced weekly attacks by 4.0 versus 4.6 for placebo over weeks 1-2. The drug performed WORSE than placebo, meaning it completely failed its primary endpoint.
all_trials.patient_relevance: Eptinezumab does not work for episodic CH -- placebo actually did better, which is a clear negative result.

all_trials.title: Cluster Headache Treatment With Rimegepant
all_trials.what_tested: Rimegepant (Nurtec), a CGRP receptor blocker pill, as a preventive treatment for cluster headache.
all_trials.key_result: Completed with 20 participants but no results posted yet.
all_trials.patient_relevance: If an oral CGRP blocker works for CH prevention, it would be much more convenient than injections or infusions -- results are awaited.

all_trials.title: Efficacy of Erenumab in Chronic Cluster Headache
all_trials.what_tested: Erenumab (Aimovig), a CGRP receptor antibody, as a treatment for chronic cluster headache.
all_trials.key_result: Completed with 101 participants but no results posted yet.
all_trials.patient_relevance: Another CGRP antibody being tested specifically for chronic CH -- results could expand treatment options if positive, given galcanezumab failed for chronic CH.

all_trials.title: A Study to Explore the Long-Term Safety and Efficacy of Fremanezumab (TEV-48125) for the Prevention of Cluster Headache
all_trials.sponsor: Teva Branded Pharmaceutical Products R&D, Inc.
all_trials.what_tested: Long-term safety and efficacy of fremanezumab (Ajovy) at various doses for preventing cluster headache (extension study of earlier trials).
all_trials.key_result: Safety-focused study: adverse events occurred in most participants across all dose groups. No efficacy endpoints were primary. The parent trials were terminated, making this extension study less meaningful.
all_trials.patient_relevance: Fremanezumab's entire CH program was discontinued -- this drug is not coming to market for cluster headache.

all_trials.title: A Study to Evaluate the Efficacy and Safety of TEV-48125 (Fremanezumab) for the Prevention of Episodic Cluster Headache (ECH)
all_trials.sponsor: Teva Branded Pharmaceutical Products R&D, Inc.
all_trials.what_tested: Fremanezumab (Ajovy) at two dose levels versus placebo for preventing episodic cluster headache attacks.
all_trials.key_result: The higher dose (900/225/225mg) reduced weekly attacks by 7.6 vs 5.7 for placebo, but the lower dose (675mg) showed essentially no difference from placebo (5.8 vs 5.7). Study was terminated.
all_trials.patient_relevance: Fremanezumab failed for episodic CH -- the small signal in one dose group was not enough to continue development.

all_trials.title: A Study Comparing the Efficacy and Safety of Fremanezumab (TEV-48125) for the Prevention of Chronic Cluster Headache (CCH)
all_trials.sponsor: Teva Branded Pharmaceutical Products R&D, Inc.
all_trials.what_tested: Fremanezumab (Ajovy) at two dose levels versus placebo for preventing chronic cluster headache attacks.
all_trials.key_result: Placebo reduced monthly attacks by 12.2, which was actually better than the lower fremanezumab dose (8.7). The higher dose (900/225/225mg) reduced attacks by 15.5 but the massive placebo response made results uninterpretable. Study terminated.
all_trials.patient_relevance: Fremanezumab failed for chronic CH too -- the huge placebo response (12 fewer monthly attacks) wiped out any signal from the drug.

all_trials.title: The Effects of Levcromakalim in Patients With Cluster Headache
all_trials.what_tested: Whether opening ATP-sensitive potassium channels (using levcromakalim) triggers cluster headache attacks, to understand the underlying disease mechanism.
all_trials.key_result: Completed with 41 participants but no results posted. This was a provocation study, not a treatment trial.
all_trials.patient_relevance: This is basic science research -- if potassium channels trigger CH attacks, drugs that block them could become future treatments.

all_trials.title: CGRP's Cluster Headache Inducing Abilities in Cluster Headache Patients
all_trials.what_tested: Whether IV infusion of CGRP (the protein targeted by new migraine drugs) can trigger cluster headache attacks in patients during active and remission phases.
all_trials.key_result: Completed with 37 participants but no results posted. This provocation study tested the role of CGRP in triggering CH.
all_trials.patient_relevance: Understanding whether CGRP directly causes CH attacks helps explain why some CGRP drugs work and others do not -- foundational research.

all_trials.title: Pain Biomarker Study
all_trials.sponsor: The University of Texas Health Science Center, Houston
all_trials.what_tested: Using capsaicin (hot pepper extract) and ice water to study pain biomarkers in headache patients, and testing whether oxygen reduces capsaicin-induced eye watering to understand how oxygen therapy works.
all_trials.key_result: Enrolling by invitation, with 371 participants. Expected completion May 2028. This is a mechanism/biomarker study.
all_trials.patient_relevance: This research could explain WHY oxygen works for CH -- understanding the mechanism could lead to better treatments.
